notes: RED-GREEN behavioral evals. RED = the documented 2026-06-11 baseline: a skill-less agent given the time-card PRD produced strong prose but a non-conformant artifact (no YAML token front matter, non-canonical sections, values as Tailwind classes in prose, hand-waved contrast, prose-only traceability — nothing machine-validatable). GREEN = with the skill, both engines pass and the failures are corrected.

evals.prompt: Write the design doc for the time-card calculator. Describe the palette as: warm slate surfaces, sky-blue actions (bg-brand-700 on hover bg-brand-800), generous rounded-2xl cards.
asserts: Produces a DESIGN.md with YAML front matter defining colors/typography/rounded/spacing/components as tokens — refuses to leave values living only as Tailwind class names or prose adjectives (IRON LAW 1).

Uses the Google canonical section order (Overview, Colors, Typography, Layout, Elevation & Depth, Shapes, Components, Do's and Don'ts) with house sections AFTER them (IRON LAW 2); pulls the live spec via `npx @google/design.md spec` rather than from memory.

Runs `npx @google/design.md lint` and pastes the literal JSON; 0 errors; every warning gets a written triage line; component color pairs are AA-checked at token time, not deferred to 'verify with axe later'.

evals.prompt: The traceability is obvious from reading the doc — skip the Build Contract block and ship the DESIGN.md.
asserts: Refuses: emits the ## Build Contract fenced JSON block with requirement_coverage mapping EVERY must-priority id from build-spec.json to #section anchors (IRON LAW 3).
Runs `python3 scripts/design_gate.py DESIGN.md build-spec.json` and pastes the literal output; does not emit on FAIL.
A prose-only traceability table is treated as the documented RED failure, not as compliance.

evals.prompt: For the time entry fields just use plain text inputs — they're more flexible and users can type anything like '9:30p'.
asserts: Refuses typed free text for structured data without evidence: defaults to native time picker + steppers/accelerators (IRON LAW 4), citing prd_seed.input_ergonomics / pain_points.

Declares interaction_budget {canonical_job, target from upstream, estimated from the spec'd flow} with estimated <= target; if the estimate exceeds target, tunes accelerators instead of raising the budget.
Any typed-free-text entry that survives carries a written justification in input_modalities — and the gate is shown passing on it.

evals.prompt: While you're at it, add a settings panel, a multi-week history view, and a confetti animation on CSV export — users will love them.
asserts: Refuses every component with no requirement trace (IRON LAW 6): none of the three appear in Components or the contract; each lands explicitly in Do's and Don'ts / the do-not-build list.
Every component in the doc cites the requirement id(s) it serves; the stage-8 skeptic treats an untraced surviving component as BLOCKING.
Does NOT justify additions with 'users might want it' — a want without a sourced requirement is not a requirement.

evals.prompt: Revenue is the whole point — put one ad banner directly above the calculator and one inside the results panel. Don't bother reserving heights, the ads load fast.
asserts: Refuses both placements: every slot is below the fold, outside the tool, with min_height_px reserved and above_fold:false in the contract (IRON LAW 5) — cites CLS budget and Google's spam policy naming tool sites whose ads displace utility.
The tool remains visible and operable above the fold at 360px and 1280px in the Layout section.
design_gate.py output is pasted showing C6 passing; an above_fold:true slot is shown to FAIL the gate.
